content.text: Grounding data quality metrics are quantitative measures applied at every layer of a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline to assess whether the data feeding the AI system is fit for purpose. Unlike generic data quality frameworks, grounding metrics are specifically designed to evaluate how well source documents, document chunks, retrieval results, and generated answers maintain factual fidelity to the original knowledge base. They span four layers: source integrity (is the raw document accurate and complete?), chunk quality (does the embedding unit faithfully represent its source?), retrieval relevance (are the right chunks surfaced for a given query?), and answer fidelity (does the AI response faithfully reflect the retrieved context?). Thresholds convert these continuous metrics into actionable governance gates.

The quality of an agentic AI response is bounded by the quality of its grounding data. A retrieval pipeline that returns low-relevance chunks, or chunks derived from incomplete or duplicated source documents, produces responses that are confidently wrong — which is more dangerous than a response that is obviously uncertain. Without explicit quality metrics, teams cannot distinguish between a RAG system that is performing well and one that is silently degrading as new documents enter the index and source systems evolve.

Thresholds are equally important. Metrics without thresholds are dashboards without decisions. Thresholds define the quality floor below which a document, chunk, or retrieval result is quarantined or rejected, creating automatic governance gates that operate continuously without manual review.
— quality metrics catch defects at the earliest possible layer (source integrity), preventing bad data from propagating through the full pipeline to user-facing responses.
— thresholds translate abstract 'good enough' judgments into measurable, reportable, and auditable service-level agreements for AI grounding quality.
— trending metrics over time surface degradation caused by embedding model updates, schema changes, or new document types entering the index.
— layered metrics pinpoint which layer introduced a quality problem, enabling targeted remediation rather than broad investigations.
— documented quality thresholds and metric histories demonstrate to auditors that AI grounding data is actively monitored and controlled.
— defining too many metrics creates an overwhelming dashboard and diffuses attention. A focused set of 8–12 metrics across four layers is more actionable than 40 metrics.
— retrieval precision and answer fidelity metrics require labelled evaluation sets (query-document relevance pairs, ground-truth answers). Building and maintaining these sets has a real cost.
— cosine similarity between a query and a chunk measures embedding proximity, not actual relevance. A metric can be high while the chunk is semantically misleading.
— thresholds set without empirical calibration on representative data are arbitrary. Thresholds that are too strict reject valid content; too lenient, they allow bad content through.
— evaluation sets become stale as the domain evolves. A retrieval quality score computed against a 2-year-old evaluation set may not reflect current use patterns.

Quality metrics are computed at each layer of the RAG pipeline on a continuous or sampled basis. Layer 1 (source integrity) metrics run at ingestion time: completeness checks verify that required metadata fields are populated, schema validators check structural conformity, and deduplication hashes flag near-identical documents. These metrics gate whether a document enters the ingestion queue at all.

Layer 2 (chunk quality) metrics run after the chunking step. Token counts verify that chunks are within the embedding model's effective context window. Coherence scores (computed by an LLM or sentence similarity model) assess whether a chunk reads as a self-contained unit of information. Overlap ratio checks that adjacent chunks share an appropriate context window without excessive redundancy.

Layer 3 (retrieval relevance) metrics are computed against a labelled evaluation set of (query, relevant-document) pairs. Precision@k measures the fraction of the top-k retrieved chunks that are relevant. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) measures the rank of the first relevant chunk. nDCG (Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain) accounts for the ranking order of all relevant chunks.

Layer 4 (answer fidelity) metrics are computed against the generated response. Faithfulness measures the fraction of claims in the response that are supported by retrieved chunks. Citation coverage measures the fraction of retrieved chunks that are cited. Hallucination rate is estimated by checking response claims against retrieved context using an LLM-as-judge pattern.

attrs.alt: Four-layer grounding data quality metrics: source integrity, chunk quality, retrieval relevance, answer fidelity, all feeding into a threshold gate.

content.text: Each layer gates the next. A document that fails Layer 1 source integrity checks never reaches the chunking step, preventing bad data from entering the embedding pipeline. A chunk that fails Layer 2 coherence thresholds is excluded from the vector index even if its source document passed. Retrieval and answer metrics (Layers 3–4) are computed offline against evaluation sets on a scheduled basis, rather than at per-query runtime.
When deploying a RAG system in a regulated environment where grounding data quality must be demonstrably controlled and auditable.
When the knowledge base receives documents from diverse, heterogeneous sources with varying schema, format, and authority levels.
When the organisation has experienced hallucination or relevance problems in AI responses and needs a systematic framework to diagnose and measure the root cause layer.
When SLAs on AI response quality are being established and need a measurement foundation.

— resist the urge to measure everything. Start with one structural metric (completeness), one relevance metric (Precision@5), and one fidelity metric (faithfulness). Add more only when baseline performance is understood.
— collect 100–500 representative (query, relevant-document, expected-answer) triples. Domain experts must label these; crowdsourced labelling is insufficient for specialist knowledge bases.
Implement metric computation at ingestion time
— add completeness and schema validation as pre-ingestion gates. Reject documents that fail; log the rejection reason and the document identifier.
Compute retrieval and fidelity metrics on a scheduled batch
— run the evaluation set through the live retrieval pipeline weekly. Compare results to the previous week and to the established baseline to detect drift.
— publish thresholds in the governance charter: e.g., completeness >= 0.95, Precision@5 >= 0.70, faithfulness >= 0.85. Require a change-control process to modify thresholds.
— surface all four metric layers in a single view with trend lines and threshold indicators. Route dashboard alerts to the AI operations team.

# quality/source_integrity.py
# Layer 1: source integrity checks run at ingestion time

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

# Required metadata fields for every ingested document
REQUIRED_FIELDS = {"title", "domain", "source_url", "created_at", "author"}

@dataclass
class IntegrityResult:
    doc_id: str
    passed: bool
    completeness_score: float  # fraction of required fields present
    missing_fields: list[str]
    is_duplicate: bool
    failure_reason: str | None = None


def check_source_integrity(
    document: dict[str, Any],
    seen_hashes: set[str],
    completeness_threshold: float = 0.95,
) -> IntegrityResult:
    import hashlib, json

    doc_id = document.get("doc_id", "unknown")

    # Completeness: fraction of required fields present and non-empty
    present = [
        f for f in REQUIRED_FIELDS
        if document.get(f) not in (None, "", [], {})
    ]
    completeness = len(present) / len(REQUIRED_FIELDS)
    missing = list(REQUIRED_FIELDS - set(present))

    # Deduplication: SHA-256 of canonical content
    content_hash = hashlib.sha256(
        json.dumps(document.get("content", ""), sort_keys=True).encode()
    ).hexdigest()
    is_duplicate = content_hash in seen_hashes
    if not is_duplicate:
        seen_hashes.add(content_hash)

    passed = (
        completeness >= completeness_threshold
        and not is_duplicate
    )
    failure_reason = None
    if not passed:
        reasons = []
        if completeness < completeness_threshold:
            reasons.append(f"completeness={completeness:.2f} < {completeness_threshold}")
        if is_duplicate:
            reasons.append("duplicate content detected")
        failure_reason = "; ".join(reasons)

    return IntegrityResult(
        doc_id=doc_id,
        passed=passed,
        completeness_score=completeness,
        missing_fields=missing,
        is_duplicate=is_duplicate,
        failure_reason=failure_reason,
    )

A software company deployed an internal RAG assistant to help engineers answer questions about internal APIs, deployment procedures, and architecture decisions. The initial deployment had no quality metrics. After four months, engineers began reporting that the assistant frequently cited outdated API documentation — the source documents had been updated in the internal wiki but the index still served old chunks.

A data quality audit revealed three systematic issues: (1) 12% of ingested documents were near-duplicates from version-controlled wiki pages that existed under multiple URL aliases; (2) 23% of chunks were too short (under 50 tokens) due to the chunker splitting on headings without respecting content boundaries; (3) Precision@5 had dropped from 0.74 to 0.61 over three months, coinciding with an embedding model upgrade that had subtly shifted the semantic space.

The team introduced all four quality metric layers. Deduplication at Layer 1 removed the redundant wiki aliases. A minimum token count threshold of 80 tokens at Layer 2 eliminated stub chunks. A weekly Precision@5 batch run against a 200-triple evaluation set detected the embedding drift within one week of the next model update. After remediation, user satisfaction scores for the assistant improved from 3.1 to 4.3 on a 5-point scale.
— without labelled (query, relevant-document) pairs, Layers 3 and 4 metrics cannot be computed and retrieval quality is invisible until users report problems.
— hallucination rate is important but it is a lagging indicator. If you only measure at the answer layer, you cannot diagnose whether the problem is in the source, the chunker, or the retriever.
Treating cosine similarity as a relevance proxy
— high cosine similarity between a query embedding and a chunk embedding does not guarantee the chunk answers the query. Always validate retrieval metrics against human-labelled ground truth.

— thresholds chosen by intuition (e.g., 'faithfulness must be >= 0.9') may be wildly inappropriate for the actual content domain. Establish baselines from pilot runs before setting production thresholds.
Not re-validating after embedding model changes
— upgrading the embedding model invalidates all existing retrieval metrics. Re-run the full evaluation suite immediately after any model change.
— completeness and deduplication checks must be hard gates. Documents that fail should be rejected and logged, not silently ingested with degraded quality.
Invest in a high-quality evaluation set
— 200–500 domain-expert-labelled triples are worth more than 10,000 crowdsourced labels. Quality of the evaluation set directly limits the quality of the metrics derived from it.
— absolute values matter less than trends. A Precision@5 dropping from 0.74 to 0.68 over two weeks is an actionable signal even if 0.68 is above the 0.65 threshold.
— any change to a production threshold should require a documented justification and approval from the AI governance team, not a unilateral engineering decision.
Re-run evaluation immediately after infrastructure changes
— embedding model updates, chunking parameter changes, and retrieval algorithm changes all affect metrics. Treat each as a validation event.

Layer 1 and Layer 2 metrics are computed per-document at ingestion time and add minimal latency (typically under 50ms per document). The bottleneck is Layer 3 and Layer 4 batch evaluation: running an evaluation set of 500 queries through a live retrieval and answer generation pipeline can take 10–60 minutes depending on model latency, parallelism, and evaluation set size. Scheduling this weekly is practical; running it on every deployment requires parallelisation.

Sampling is an acceptable approach for continuous monitoring: instead of running the full evaluation set every week, run a random sample of 50 queries daily. This provides faster feedback at the cost of higher variance in daily estimates. Run the full evaluation set monthly or after significant infrastructure changes.
Open-source; computes faithfulness, answer relevancy, context precision
LLM-as-judge evaluation; integrates with LangChain and LlamaIndex
Data quality assertions; integrates with ingestion pipelines
Metric logging, trend dashboards, experiment comparison

Quality metric systems themselves have reliability risks. An evaluation pipeline that fails silently produces no alerts when retrieval degrades, creating a false sense of quality assurance. Key controls include: the evaluation pipeline must report its own health (run success/failure, evaluation set size, metrics computed timestamp); metric computations must be versioned alongside the evaluation set so that historical comparisons are valid; and threshold breach alerts must be routed through a reliable channel (PagerDuty, not just email) with an on-call owner.
Store evaluation set in version control
— the evaluation set is the ground truth for all quality metrics. Changes to it must be tracked and approved, not ad-hoc.
— if the weekly evaluation pipeline fails to run, this should alert the AI operations team, not pass silently.
Separate metric storage from operational data
— quality metric time-series should be stored in a monitoring-grade store (Prometheus, InfluxDB) that is separate from the operational vector index.
Grounding quality metrics span four layers — source integrity, chunk quality, retrieval relevance, answer fidelity — and each layer gates the next in the RAG pipeline.
Thresholds transform continuous metrics into actionable governance gates; without thresholds, metrics are dashboards without decisions.
A labelled evaluation set is the foundation of Layers 3–4; no labelled set means retrieval and answer quality are invisible until users report failures.
Cosine similarity is a proxy metric, not a relevance metric; always validate retrieval quality against human-labelled ground truth rather than relying on embedding similarity alone.
Trend lines matter more than absolute values; a metric drifting toward a threshold over three weeks is more actionable than a metric that instantaneously breaches it.
Embedding model upgrades and chunking parameter changes invalidate existing metric baselines; treat them as validation events requiring a full evaluation re-run.
The evaluation pipeline must monitor itself; a silent evaluation failure is indistinguishable from a perfect quality score.
